[189:39]much more positive. Finally, some good news. So when Trump ran for office in 2016, he launched

[189:49]a revolutionary America first agenda in complete contravention of the mainstream Republican

[189:58]The old Republican agenda was based on cutting taxes, shrinking government,

[190:04]deregulating the economy, going to war for Israel, among other things.

[190:09]Trump's agenda was based on three things.

[190:12]Ending the foreign wars, a foreign policy of restraint,

[190:16]It was based on immigration restriction, preserving the identity of America, its cultural identity,

[190:23]its heritage, its demographics, the integrity of its laws and its borders.

[190:28]And lastly, although this is the one that people don't talk about as much, but we're

[190:32]talking about it tonight, one of the fundamental biggest pillars of Trumpism of America first

[190:39]trade policy and industrial policy. This is quintessential. This is fundamental to nationalism.

[190:48]It is fundamental to Trumpism, to America first. And the reason I frame it this way

[190:55]is because a lot has changed since Trump launched his first campaign 10 years ago.

[191:03]It seems that Trump is fighting

[191:07]To have a restrained foreign policy and losing that fight. We're bombing Yemen. We're threatening Iran

[191:15]We're giving Israel weapons to destroy Gaza. We're unhappy with Putin and maybe getting more aggressive against Russia

[191:22]So there's this eternal struggle

[191:25]to restrain the American war machine because of the military industrial complex because of the Zionists the neocons the Jews

[191:35]So, we have not quite achieved that.

[191:38]American troops remain in Iraq, remain in Syria, we're actually deploying more force.

[191:44]It's a greater force posture in the Middle East than before he got in.

[191:49]On immigration, once again, it's a fight.

[191:54]We have secured the border, but there is no border wall.

[191:57]We have secured the border, but there are no mass deportations.

[192:01]We have not reversed all of the illegals that have poured in under Biden, which was even

[192:06]greater than anything before Trump first came to office in 2016.

[192:25]we want Americans to be dreamers,

[192:27]we want Americans to be programmers.

[192:29]Now, Trump wants more H1Bs.

[192:35]this is just a really quick aside,

[192:37]it has nothing to do with the show tonight.

[194:57]because it's like, that's not true.

[195:17]Anyway, but to return to my point,

[195:21]So, Trump is needless to say, struggling on immigration.

[195:25]But the one thing that Trump has been consistently solid on, this is my point, if Trump has been

[195:31]unsuccessful at implementing a restrained America first foreign policy, if he's been

[195:37]unsuccessful at implementing an immigration restriction policy.

[195:42]What he has been successful at, I think his, by far and away, the biggest success is that

[195:48]he is solidly America first on trade, always has been.

[195:52]This has always been the most solid part.

[195:56]And Trumpism is a revolutionary political movement.

[196:01]And I should say it has become really a reforming movement.

[196:11]He's not genuinely created regime change.

[196:14]He's not genuinely really changed

[196:18]the political system of the United States.

[196:20]He's changed characteristics of it,

[196:22]but he hasn't fundamentally changed it.

[196:24]It's not in the truest sense of revolution.

[196:27]It started out that way,

[196:28]and then it became a reforming movement.

[196:31]And with any reform movement,

[196:33]the regime, the political order,

[196:35]accepts

[196:36]some of the tenets of the revolution and rejects others.

[196:40]That's why it's a reform and not a complete upheaval.

[196:44]And that doesn't mean it's a failure.

[196:47]That doesn't mean necessarily the Trump revolution is a failure.

[196:49]We have to judge it based on a net

[196:52]which reforms have been accepted by the system.

[196:55]And I think one of the big reforms that was accepted by the system

[196:59]was that free trade is not working.

[197:03]That is one of those things

[197:05]that was undeniably absolutely true.

[197:08]Which is that free trade is bankrupting America. He was right. And the system recognizes that.

[197:15]How do we know? Because when Trump won in 2016, free trade was the universal dogma of the political system.

[197:24]We want free trade. We want bilateral or multilateral free trade agreements.

[197:29]Obama was implementing the TTP, the TTIP.

[197:33]Among others, we had NAFTA in place.

[197:36]Nobody was arguing against NAFTA since the year 2000, you know, since the reform party.

[197:43]And Trump turned that all on its head.

[197:46]And after four years of Trump from 2017 to 2021, Biden kept in place Trump's tariffs

[197:54]on China.

[197:56]So, Trump entered where free trade was the undisputed dogma, and by the time he left,

[198:02]his successor kept in place the protectionist tariffs.

[198:06]And not only that, but he actually made the industrial policy more comprehensive.

[198:13]Not only did he keep in place the trade barriers, but he also created the CHIPS Act and the

[198:26]And we actually got a lot of investment from Europe

[198:29]because of the Biden infrastructure law

[198:31]or the Inflation Reduction Act also.

[198:34]And the CHIPS Act, although it's not enough,

[198:37]is starting to bring manufacturing back.

[198:40]It's starting to bring back some industry

[198:43]to the United States.

[198:44]So you have had, like I said, this reform has been accepted over what will then be 12

[198:51]years, four years of Trump, four years of Biden, four more years of Trump.

[198:54]If it is successful, it will continue on after that.

[198:57]And so I want to make that clear because a lot of people say you're very negative about

[199:01]Trump some days and other days you're positive.

[199:05]Well, I want to make it clear.

[199:07]I don't think holding for federal money hostage

[199:11]from elite universities to change

[199:15]what their professors say about Israel.

[199:17]I don't think that's a good thing.

[199:18]I don't think it's a good thing to bomb Iran.

[199:20]I don't think it's a good thing

[199:22]to drag our feet on deportations.

[199:24]It is a good thing to implement trade barriers and tariffs.

[199:28]I support that 100%.

[199:29]And I think Trump has always been very successful at that.

[199:33]I think the regime is

[199:34]in some ways accepting this reform. But the big development from today, as we finally got the

[199:40]big tariff package, Trump campaigned throughout the election last year on a 15% across the

[199:47]board tariff, which is all goods coming into the United States would be taxed at 15%. And I thought

[199:54]this was almost unrealistically too good. And I was very critical last week.

[200:01]It sounded like Trump was walking it back.

[200:03]For the past three months, Trump has been sort of hemming and hawing about what that

[200:08]promise would actually become in policy.

[200:12]He said he would do a 15% across the board tariff, and then once elected and then inaugurated,

[200:18]there was this open discussion, this sort of open process where he pondered whether it

[200:29]He floated the idea of a reciprocal tariff which is that countries with high barriers,

[200:36]we would impose a high barrier on them.

[200:38]Countries with low barriers, we'd impose low or no barrier on them.

[200:42]And then last week, he said, well, maybe we'll just be super flexible.

[200:46]We're gonna be reciprocal, but we're gonna be super flexible.

[200:49]And so he was saying all these different things at once.

[200:52]He would say, well, we're gonna be reciprocal and very tough,

[200:55]but also we have to be flexible, but not too flexible.

[200:58]We've been flexible for too long,

[201:00]but also it's very important to be flexible.

[201:03]So we didn't know what it would be.

[201:05]But Trump said that today, April 2nd would be Liberation Day.

[201:10]This is when he would roll out his trade plan.

[201:12]And according to the news, according to some sources,

[201:15]he came to his final conclusion, to a decision only yesterday.

[201:22]So he was deciding, apparently, for months.

[201:26]And then yesterday, the day before, made the decision which plan he would go with.

[201:32]And the plan is unbelievably positive.

[201:36]It is very broad, very far reaching, very tough,

[201:40]very aggressive, surprisingly so.

[201:43]And I'm very pleasantly surprised with the package.

[201:46]So today, Trump announced reciprocal tariffs

[201:49]on almost every country in the world.

[201:54]It's a two-part policy, like I said earlier.

[201:58]The first part is it is a 10% across the board universal permanent baseline tariff.

[202:07]It's the baseline.

[202:08]That means that the floor for all imports coming into the United States are going to

[202:14]be taxed at 10% no matter what.

[202:17]That's huge.

[202:18]It's huge that it's a baseline, that it's across the board.

[202:24]And we'll talk about why that is, but that is part number one, is that it's a 10% baseline

[202:29]tariff for everybody.

[202:32]On top of that, for those countries with trade barriers against the United States, for those

[202:38]countries like China, like Korea, like the European Union, like others, those countries

[202:48]In excess of 10% against our goods,

[202:51]we are adding an additional tariff on top of the 10%

[202:55]in proportion to how high their barriers are

[202:58]against the United States.

[203:00]So it's a baseline 10% and it's proportionate

[203:03]against those countries with higher trade barriers

[203:06]against the United States.

[203:08]And this is a story from the New York Times.

[203:10]It says, quote, President Trump unveiled

[203:13]his most expansive tariffs to date.

[203:16]saying he will impose a 10% tariff on all trading partners except Canada and Mexico as well as double-digit tariffs on dozens of other countries.

[203:25]While Mr. Trump has been saying for weeks he would impose reciprocal tariffs, his announcement

[203:30]went far beyond what many analysts had expected.

[203:33]The tariffs will apply to more than 100 trading partners, including the European Union, China,

[203:39]Britain, India.

[203:40]Under Mr. Trump's plan, the United States will impose a staggering new 34% tariff on

[203:46]Chinese goods.

[203:53]A 20% tariff on imports from the European Union, and a 24% tariff on goods from Japan.

[203:59]India will face a 26% tariff on its exports to the United States.

[204:05]Business groups, trade experts, economists, democratic, and even a few Republican lawmakers

[204:10]swiftly rebuke the tariffs.

[204:13]Officials from other countries protested with some contemplating retaliation.

[204:18]In a slight reprieve, the new tariffs will not apply to products that Mr. Trump already

[204:22]hit with separate levies, including steel and aluminum and vehicles and vehicle parts.

[204:27]Energy and other minerals not available in the United States will also be excluded.

[204:33]White House officials defended the tariffs by arguing that pernicious trading practices

[204:37]by other countries led to large and persistent trade deficits for the United States.

[204:42]And senior administration officials said they had little appetite for haggling with individual

[204:47]countries over lower tariff rates, even with U.S. allies that have offered to reduce their

[204:52]own levies on American exports in recent days.

[204:55]Trump officials also issued an early warning to countries that have threatened to impose

[205:14]of the country.

[205:15]And it absolutely cements President Trump's legacy that he is trying to usher in a golden

[205:20]age of economy, production and prosperity.

[205:24]So this policy is great.

[205:29]Like I said, totally blew away my wildest expectations.

[205:33]It could have gone further.

[205:35]I think the baseline tariff could be a little higher.

[205:38]That group in particular CPA,

[205:41]their policy paper recommends a 17% baseline tariff.

[205:46]And Trump promised 15%.

[205:48]So, you know, it could have been a little bit higher.

[205:51]But overall, this is an extremely bold

[205:54]and unprecedented trade action.

[205:58]want to restate this point because this is very important. These were my concerns about the trade

[206:04]policy, which have now been answered. The first is that so far, Trump's implementation of tariffs

[206:10]has been scattershot. He is creating a climate of uncertainty, unpredictability, threatens tariffs,

[206:18]then walks it back. Says we'll use tariffs to achieve very ambiguous concessions from other

[206:26]It's hard to say objectively how those conditions would be satisfied.

[206:31]And therefore, it's very hard to predict whether there will be tariffs and against what countries

[206:36]and on what goods.

[206:39]And the problem with that scattershot approach, without predictability, without certainty,

[206:46]you cannot have an economic correction.

[206:53]multinational corporations and other economic decision makers, if they know

[206:58]that there's gonna be a tariff, it's gonna be this much, and this is when it's

[207:02]going into effect, they can plan. If they have confidence knowing when it's gonna

[207:08]happen, how much it's gonna be, how long it will last, when and how it will apply

[207:13]against what goods, they can make long-term decisions with confidence.

[207:20]about where they're going to deploy capital.

[207:23]But if they don't know, then they can't.

[207:27]They can't make those decisions.

[207:29]And therefore they will be hesitant to deploy capital.

[207:33]They'll be hesitant, for example,

[207:35]to invest money in America if they don't know.

[207:37]And so that's not the best thing.

[207:41]It needs to be permanent, predictable, reliable.

[207:45]It needs to be certain so that this recalibration can occur so that companies can make these

[207:50]decisions with confidence.

[207:52]We can change the parameters of the economy, but we need to do it in such a way where people

[207:57]know what to expect.

[207:59]And there will be a period of correction and recalibration.

[208:03]And that is to be expected.

[208:05]But over a period of time, after the transition,

[208:08]there will be a new normal that is established.

[208:10]That can only happen in an environment

[208:12]where there's predictability and certainty.

[208:14]So that's one.

[208:15]Two, and this is my other concern,

[208:18]is that Trump uses tariffs, not just for the economy,

[208:21]but also for foreign policy.

[208:23]It's one thing to say, we're gonna put in place tariffs.

[208:27]permanently. That is the new law of the land. America has trade barriers now and everyone's

[208:32]going to have to deal with it. And we're doing it to grow our economy. It's something completely

[208:37]different to say, we're going to put tariffs on you if you don't do what we say. But if

[208:43]you do it, we're going to let him go. That is a foreign policy tool, not an economic

[208:48]tool. That is meant to compel compliance with the State Department.

[208:54]It's not necessarily meant to grow American industry,

[208:57]strictly speaking.

[208:59]And that's another issue.

[209:01]We need permanent tariffs.

[209:03]And those two issues are related

[209:05]because you can understand if they're being used

[209:07]as a negotiating tool,

[209:09]then they're gonna be ad hoc.

[209:11]Then they're gonna be unpredictable.

[209:13]Then they're gonna be off and on.

[209:16]We're gonna threaten them and then walk it back,

[209:17]adjust them.

[209:19]So, what we need are tariffs for our economy.

[209:22]We need them permanently.

[209:23]We need them to be consistent, predictable.

[209:25]We need them to be a part of a mix of other policies that come from the government.

[209:32]Yes, the government will need to take a role in planning the economy to some extent, although

[209:37]that is anathema.

[209:39]We need the government to steer the economy using tariffs as part of a broader policy

[209:47]nurture domestic industry. Now, a lot of people say that kind of thinking is

[209:53]socialist or communist. They fall back on these heuristics. They say the

[209:59]government can't do anything right. The government is inefficient. Innovation

[210:02]comes from the free market. The free market allocates resources efficiently.

[210:07]The free market organizes the economy efficiently. The government can't do it.

[210:12]Now, these are heuristics and these are ideas which are true within context.

[210:20]They're partially true.

[210:23]The price mechanism, for example, the idea that we have prices, why does something cost

[210:29]a certain amount of money?

[210:38]If there's an egg shortage, for example,

[210:41]eggs are gonna become more expensive

[210:43]because there's fewer of them.

[210:46]There's fewer of them, which means demand for them increases.

[210:49]Not everybody can have as many eggs as they want.

[210:52]We're going to have to economize on them.

[210:54]We're going to have to constrain how they're allocated.

[210:59]We raise the price and only those people

[211:01]that absolutely need them will be able to get them

[211:04]because they'll be willing to pay for them.

[211:05]That is what the price signal is good at.

[211:08]And markets, insofar as that is letting the price mechanism do its thing,

[211:14]having markets without a lot of barriers,

[211:16]letting prices communicate relative scarcity to allocate resources,

[211:20]generally that's a good thing.

[211:24]But that by itself is not the only consideration in an economy.

[211:30]That is true within limitations.

[211:34]One of the reasons, for example, one of the shortfalls of the market, of the private sector, of the price system,

[211:45]is that the price system, the markets are not always necessarily the best at long-term planning,

[211:53]because

[211:55]Maybe that works in time.

[211:57]Maybe that works right now.

[211:59]If you wanna reflect the relative scarcity

[212:02]of something like eggs right now,

[212:04]well, the price system will communicate

[212:07]the relative scarcity of the eggs

[212:09]with how the prices rise and fall.

[212:11]But over the long term,

[212:14]the market has difficulty doing these things,

[212:16]especially with things like technology

[212:19]that don't yet exist.

[212:25]human flight, the internet, green technologies,

[212:29]new sources of energy.

[212:32]They might not necessarily be economical right now.

[212:37]There might have to be, for example,

[212:39]in a new technology, which might be very risky,

[212:43]might have very low odds of becoming viable.

[212:47]And then therefore lucrative,

[212:49]it might not necessarily be

[212:52]According to the laws of the market, profitable to pour tons of money in research and development

[212:58]on a very risky, very speculative new type of technology.

[213:03]That is why throughout the history of the United States, many of the biggest technological

[213:08]innovations have come from the military.

[213:11]So the internet is a perfect example.

[213:21]And other tech products, they would not have come into existence without huge government

[213:28]money for a military application, tax dollars that were spent.

[213:36]with no necessarily no anticipation of a short-term profit

[213:40]or maybe even a profit at all.

[213:42]Something like the Manhattan Project, the nuclear bomb

[213:45]could have never been created by the private sector.

[213:48]It just wouldn't have happened.

[213:49]The nuclear bomb was created by the government.

[213:52]The internet was created by the government.

[213:56]The first computers created by the government.

[213:58]Many of these things were created,

[214:01]the resources were marshaled and directed by the government.

[214:05]So this these kinds of here and these are examples, but these types of heuristics like

[214:11]Innovation only comes from the private sector

[214:14]That's not necessarily true or wholly true

[214:18]the idea that markets

[214:20]perfectly

[214:22]Organize our society for us is not necessarily always true

[214:27]Or at least it's partially true

[214:30]And so that's one of the first things to understand

[214:32]about a protectionist policy.

[214:34]When you start to say we need the government

[214:36]to subsidize industries, protect industries,

[214:39]we need the government to actually plan for the future.

[214:43]We need the government to think 10, 20, 30 years

[214:46]in the future.

[214:49]allocate resources based on a long-term plan, you get conservatives throw up

[214:54]their hands and say, that's socialism, that's central planning, that doesn't

[214:58]work. Hayek and Mises said that doesn't work. Well, actually, it does work.

[215:05]It worked in the case of the Manhattan Project.

[215:08]It worked in the case of the internet.

[215:10]It worked in the case of building up our industry in World War II.

[215:14]It worked in the case of the tariffs that were in place

[215:16]from the founding of the country until 1930.

[215:21]Actually, it does work.

[215:24]So that's just some of the common arguments from conservatives about tariffs in general

[215:29]or a protective and industrial policy in general.

[215:32]But then people talk about tariffs, it's so funny.

[215:35]You look at this announcement about the tariffs and it's everybody in elite society is freaking

[215:42]out.

[215:43]It's Republicans and Democrats.

[215:50]It's liberals and conservatives are all saying, Oh, Trump's trade policies are so dumb. Oh, I don't understand what he's doing.

[215:57]This goes too far. This is going to shrink the economy. This is going to cause a recession.

[216:02]This is going to raise prices.

[216:05]To put it very simply,

[216:08]the problem that they have

[216:11]with the tariffs is it means that it's going to be less profits for them.

[216:17]It will be less profits for giant multinational corporations.

[216:22]They will undergo some years of pain.

[216:25]Those that own stocks,

[216:28]of which only the richest people own most of the stocks,

[216:31]it is those that own the assets, those that own stocks,

[216:34]from which all this foreign money pours into,

[216:37]or to which all the foreign money is pouring into.

[216:39]It is they.

[216:40]Who will undergo a period of pain when the stock market goes down for a little while when the economy contracts for a little while and

[216:48]It is those media companies that they own which are sending the message

[216:52]It is the political parties that they contribute to which are complaining in a word. It is the capitalist class

[217:01]It is those that own what is now considered to be capital

[217:05]It is those with wealth.

[217:08]Most people in this country do not have wealth.

[217:11]The vast majority of the people in this country

[217:14]do not have wealth.

[217:16]We say about most people, they live paycheck to paycheck.

[217:19]What that means is if they lost their job and their income,

[217:24]they have no savings to fall back on.

[217:26]They don't even have cash holdings.

[217:28]They don't even have cash.

[217:30]They don't own their homes.

[217:32]They don't own stocks.

[217:34]They don't own businesses.

[217:37]They don't even have cash in the bank.

[217:39]They have no wealth.

[217:41]And there is all the difference in the world,

[217:43]in this economy, between those that have wealth,

[217:46]not even those that make money, that make income,

[217:48]but those that own wealth and those that do not.

[217:52]Those that own stocks, those that own real estate,

[217:57]those that own companies, firms,

[218:01]It is they and the richest among them that own the media.

[218:05]It is they that contribute money to the political system.

[218:08]And it is they that are the most affected by this.

[218:12]Because what our trade policy effectively does is it sucks money out of the United States.

[218:20]It sucks money out of industry, out of manufacturing, out of small towns inside the United States.

[218:28]It sucks it out into the world.

[218:31]And it is returned back to the United States in the form of foreign investment, in the

[218:41]foreign companies or entities buying real estate services,

[218:47]insurance services, financial services.

[218:51]We have financialized the economy.

[218:54]The wealth and the money is leaving

[218:56]the productive parts of our economy.

[218:58]It's leaving factories.

[219:00]It's leaving manufacturing plants.

[219:02]It's leaving automakers.

[219:04]It's going out into the United States

[219:06]like a wave receding, like the ocean pulling back.

[219:09]And then it comes back into the United States,

[219:11]into the financial industry, the insurance industry,

[219:15]the real estate industry.

[219:16]I'll talk about how that happens, but that is effectively what is taking place.

[219:21]It's a wealth transfer.

[219:22]And so over the past 20 or 30 years, the proportion of economic activity in the United States

[219:28]that is manufacturing, that is industry, that is agriculture has gone down and the amount

[219:35]of the economy that is services, financial insurance and real estate has gone up.

[219:43]The money went from good paying jobs, factories dispersed across society, across the country.

[219:50]The money has left. The wealth has been transferred into financial services, real estate services, insurance services in New York, in Chicago, in the big cities of San Francisco and LA,

[220:03]and has rewarded those people that are in those industries.

[220:08]and it rewards those that own assets

[220:11]where the money is flowing into.

[220:13]If you own real estate,

[220:14]this money is coming into real estate,

[220:16]it makes your wealth more valuable.

[220:18]If you own stocks, the money's leaving industry,

[220:20]going out of the world,

[220:21]flowing back as a form of foreign investment

[220:23]into companies, into stocks, it makes the stocks go up.

[220:28]So if you own, if you own, okay,

[220:32]if you have wealth, if you have,

[220:35]If you're a capitalist, if you have capital, you are a happy camper.

[220:41]You are benefiting from this trade policy.

[220:43]You are being enriched.

[220:46]But if you're an income earner, if you're a worker, you are being impoverished.

[220:53]You are having your wealth taken.

[220:55]Your job is gone.

[220:57]And that is causing a death spiral.

[221:00]It's causing your whole society to collapse in on itself

[221:04]in all the midsize and smaller towns across the country.

[221:08]That is why

[221:10]Both sides are articulating dissatisfaction.

[221:14]That's why both sides, Republicans and Democrats,

[221:17]the Wall Street Journal and the Washington Post

[221:19]and the New York Times are saying, this is terrible.

[221:23]That's why both sides, because they're the same side.

[221:26]Because the rich, the capitalists, the wealth owners,

[221:30]they also happen to own the media.

[221:32]They also happen to own the political system.

[221:34]with everything else they own.

[221:36]And they're squealing out

[221:37]because when this trade policy happens,

[221:40]it will reverse that process.

[221:42]And these profits and the foreign investment

[221:45]that's coming in is gonna start to dry up.

[221:49]And it's gonna be at the minimum a headache.

[221:51]It may be a loss for them.

[221:53]They may lose money on this.

[221:54]They will lose money in this transitional period.

[221:57]And that's why they're sort of leaving out that part of it.

[222:01]They're saying this will be very bad.

[222:04]But they're leaving out the corollary, which is for me.

[222:08]This will be very bad for us, not for the country.

[222:12]It will be bad for them.

[222:13]It will be bad for multinational corporations.

[222:16]It will be bad for the wealthy.

[222:18]It will be bad for the capitalists.

[222:21]It may be bad for them in the short term.

[222:24]How does this happen?

[222:32]What is a trade deficit?

[222:34]It means that we are importing more than we are exporting.

[222:39]So on net, the whole country against all the other countries,

[222:44]we import more than we export overall.

[222:48]But we also maintain persistent high trade deficits

[222:51]with our biggest trading partners bilaterally,

[222:54]like China, like India, like the European Union,

[222:57]and so on.

[222:58]What is a trade deficit?

[223:00]It means that when we do trade with China, we get more stuff than we give them.

[223:06]We on net are buying more stuff from China than they are buying from us.

[223:11]The deficit is the difference.

[223:13]So we're getting stuff.

[223:16]We're getting more than we're giving.

[223:19]Well, no country on earth gives more than they get for free.

[223:29]On net, everything has to balance out to zero.

[223:32]So, if we're getting more than we're giving,

[223:37]it's not that we're getting these goods from China for free.

[223:40]We're not getting this glut of manufactured products for nothing.

[223:44]What a sweet deal.

[223:45]We get all this stuff and we don't have to give them that much stuff in return.

[223:49]Well, it's just in a different account.

[223:52]It has to square to zero in a different account.

[223:54]Otherwise, it wouldn't be profitable for countries to engage in trade.

[223:58]So, if we're getting more than we're giving to China, it's not that we're actually getting

[224:06]a surplus of stuff, we're just paying for it with things other than stuff.

[224:11]We're paying for it with things other than strictly goods and services.

[224:17]How do we pay for things other than in goods and services?

[224:25]And the cash eventually flows into foreign investment in real estate stocks.

[224:31]It flows into debt.

[224:33]They buy up our debt.

[224:34]They buy up our bonds.

[224:35]They buy up our hard assets in the country.

[224:40]So when we have a trade deficit with China,

[224:42]we get all this stuff.

[224:44]We give less.

[224:46]The difference, let's say we're giving it to them in cash.

[224:50]So, you know, we import a million dollars worth of stuff.

[224:54]We export $500,000 worth of stuff.

[224:57]Then we give them $500,000 in cash.

[225:01]Chinese company puts that in a bank.

[225:03]The bank takes that $500,000 and they come back to America and they buy bonds, which

[225:08]is debt, which they earn interest on.

[225:10]We owe them.

[225:11]They buy real estate, a productive asset.

[225:16]And by the way, there's a finite quantity of real estate.

[225:19]They buy up land.

[225:20]They buy up property.

[225:23]They buy up stocks.

[225:25]They take that money and they buy stocks

[225:28]on an American stock exchange.

[225:30]And now they own equity in a company

[225:31]and they're getting a dividend.

[225:33]They're earning a rent on that as well.

[225:36]We're getting stuff, they're getting our hard assets.

[225:39]We're getting stuff, we're getting textiles,

[225:43]we're getting apparel, we're getting parts, whatever.

[225:47]And what they in return are getting is our country.

[225:50]They're getting ownership over our country.

[225:52]They're getting a claim over our debt.

[225:54]They're getting a claim over our companies,

[225:56]ownership in our firms,

[225:58]and they're getting a claim over our land.

[225:59]They're getting our real estate.

[226:02]Now, maybe you're starting to understand why the rich love free trade, why they don't

[226:08]care about deficits, why they say deficits don't matter.

[226:12]Because we're importing all these cheap goods.

[226:15]It's putting our industry out of business.

[226:18]When we're getting more stuff than we're giving, over time, we stop making stuff because

[226:24]they're making our stuff.

[226:28]We're, it's more competitive.

[226:30]We're buying all of it.

[226:31]And so we're not, it's no longer profitable

[226:33]or economical to buy from ourselves.

[226:36]So we stopped making stuff.

[226:37]So factories shut down, manufacturing shuts down.

[226:41]We don't make steel anymore.

[226:42]We don't make cars anymore.

[226:43]We don't make stuff anymore.

[226:45]They make the stuff.

[226:48]So we buy their stuff, they buy our assets.

[226:54]What happens when that money goes out to China?

[226:57]We import products from them, we give them our currency,

[227:00]they buy our assets,

[227:02]the money comes back to the United States.

[227:05]We buy their goods, the cash comes back to us.

[227:11]of a country like China owning all our shit,

[227:15]but also let's think about the effect

[227:16]that that has on the country.

[227:18]When China takes the money that we've given them

[227:20]for their stuff and they spend it here,

[227:25]Stock market goes up.

[227:26]They buy our company.

[227:28]It drives up the value of the stock market.

[227:30]When they buy real estate, it drives up the price of real estate.

[227:35]When they buy our debt, it drives down interest rates.

[227:39]And what's more, when they buy real estate, how does that happen?

[227:44]Well, they talk to a giant real estate firm.

[227:48]And so now the real estate business in America is booming because we have to do these big

[227:59]to make the real estate deal and to manage their stock portfolio and other things.

[228:04]So we need a big financial industry and then you need insurance on the real estate.

[228:10]You need insurance on whatever you buy.

[228:14]So the real estate, the real estate industry, the insurance industry, and the finance industry

[228:22]are all benefiting from this as a service.

[228:25]And all those that own the real estate and business assets are being made rich off of

[228:31]this.

[228:39]Our financial sector, our service sector, which also includes real estate, insurance,

[228:45]other stuff has been growing.

[228:47]And we have become a country that no longer makes stuff, China now makes stuff, Japan

[228:53]makes stuff, Germany makes stuff, other countries make stuff.

[228:56]We do the financial transacting of the world and ensuring those things and making those

[229:45]cars, planes, parts, have factories versus making investment devices, financial instruments,

[230:06]For a variety of reasons, economically, strategically, of course, they're not.

[230:16]ammunition, and by the way, to do all those things, you know what you need?

[230:21]To make tanks and rocket ships and things like that, or fighter jets, you need a commercial

[230:28]You need an automobile industry. You need machinists. You need a workforce that knows

[230:34]how to make stuff. You need factories that can make stuff, that make stuff commercially.

[230:42]ships. If you want to shipyard, you need lots of steel. You need a domestic steel industry.

[230:57]You're making parts. You have a skilled workforce and you have the infrastructure to make construction

[231:03]equipment, infrastructure equipment, construction equipment to make, and then ultimately military

[231:08]equipment and hardware. Or do you want the financial industry or do you want a bank?

[231:16]Do you want a bunch of people doing arbitrage speculation?

[231:22]people managing investment portfolios,

[231:25]people managing insurance.

[231:26]What is better for a country to have

[231:28]in the long term, strategically?

[231:30]And that's sort of what I'm getting at.

[231:32]It is better to have industry.

[231:35]It is better to have industry for multiple reasons.

[231:39]And maybe you could start to think about why.

[231:40]The obvious is strategy.

[231:42]It is strategically better to have industry.

[231:45]If we don't have steel,

[231:46]if we don't have commercial industry,

[231:48]then we don't have a defense industry.

[231:50]If we don't have a defense industry,

[231:52]we don't have a military.

[231:54]If we don't have a military, we're fucked.

[231:56]That's basically it.

[231:58]China can build 400 times more ships than we can.

[232:01]Want to know why?

[232:02]Because they have a commercial fleet.

[232:04]Because all their factories that they have,

[232:06]they got to put the goods on ships

[232:08]and send them to other countries for trade.

[232:12]So they make all this stuff, then they make the ships that they put the stuff on that

[232:16]they send with the other countries.

[232:17]It's not that hard to then just start making ships with guns on them.

[232:21]They make cars and make other stuff.

[232:23]Then they just start making military vehicles.

[232:26]This is what they do.

[232:28]And they have a skilled workforce and they have capital, real capital, real assets to

[232:32]do these things.

[232:33]If America doesn't have shipyards because it doesn't have commercial shipping because

[232:37]it doesn't make anything, we can't make battleships.

[232:45]What happens if there's a trade dispute?

[232:47]What happens if China wants to bully us?

[232:51]Well, now we're relying on a very fragile and super complex, incomprehensible international

[232:57]trading system where a car is really coming from like 40 different countries. What happens

[233:03]if like China blockades those countries or buys those countries off, bribes them with

[233:08]cheap credit with the Belt and Road Initiative. Then what do you do? So strategically, this

[233:13]is a big problem.

[233:14]If you don't have productive capacity,

[233:17]if you don't have industry, you don't have a military.

[233:19]If you don't have a military,

[233:20]you can't impose your will on the world.

[233:22]If you can't impose your will on the world,

[233:24]you don't have supply chains, can't make stuff.

[233:28]You see how this is circular?

[233:30]To have military might, you need economic might.

[233:33]To have economic might, you need military might.

[233:37]To keep our power going, we have to preserve our industry.

[233:42]That's a strategic reason.

[233:44]But there's also an economic reason. These technologies don't come from nowhere. Where

[233:52]do you think these various technologies were invented? They rolled from one to the other.

[233:58]There's a technology tree. So we make steel, then we make railroads.

[234:05]Then after we make railroads, we make cars.

[234:07]Then after we make cars, we make planes.

[234:09]There's a tree of technology where one technology, one industry,

[234:14]then blossoms and flowers into the next.

[234:17]Because that industry is cultivating a workforce, research and development,

[234:23]know-how, state of the art equipment.

[234:26]It has in-house all of those things, which then blossom into the next thing,

[234:31]into the next technology.

[234:35]You have vacuum tubes then they turn into televisions you have steel then it turns into rail

[234:44]It's a technology tree if we want to have the most important valuable technologies of the future

[234:49]We need to make the technologies of today. We need to have the workforce. We need to have the capital

[234:53]We need to have the industry. We need to have industry towns where this stuff is happening

[234:58]You have all of it

[235:01]control over those technologies determines the course of the future. The

[235:05]country that has the nuclear bomb is a country that owns the century. The

[235:10]country that owns the battleships, the steam engines, rail, locomotives, that's

[235:16]the country that owned the previous century and so on. The country that has

[235:22]the chips and semiconductors, the country that has artificial intelligence,

[235:31]The economic and the strategic are deeply interconnected.

[235:36]And the free market doesn't think about those things.

[235:40]The free market is a tool.

[235:42]The free market is a tool that allocates resources,

[235:45]but society still needs to make decisions

[235:49]in the long term about what is beneficial holistically.

[235:54]The free market can tell you how many eggs there are.

[235:57]The free market can tell you

[235:58]how much wood should go into a pencil.

[236:01]You know, in the famous example, but the free market will not tell you which industries

[236:06]of the future are going to pay off.

[236:08]The free market is not going to negotiate with Mexico, not to dump their steel here.

[236:13]The free market is not going to set the strategic military economic, the comprehensive priorities

[236:19]of a nation in the longterm and execute them.

[236:22]That's something only the government can do.

[236:25]That's something that only the state can do.

[236:30]It's a warped view of our history where people say, it's like, you know, the free market

[236:33]times are great and big government socialisms, when times were bad, the market and the state

[236:41]are inseparable.

[236:57]that come out of there competitive.

[241:43]And then we have a real economy,
